human_relevance_statement: This study in dogs with lymphoma provided important new information on three new cancer drugs in development for humans. The dog study helped the team determine which of the three drugs were going to be most promising for further investigation, which helps researchers prioritize new cancer drugs for study in humans.

relevant_experimental_therapeutic_interv: mTOR inhibitors rapa-logs - temsirolimus, everolimus, ridaforolimus
human_relevance_statement: This study in dogs with bone cancer provides new information on treatment approaches for humans. Bone cancer in humans is rare and affects mostly children and young adults. Dogs provide a way to study this disease and provide new insight into how it can be treated more effectively in humans.

relevant_experimental_therapeutic_interv: ["platinum-based agents such as oxaliplatin, carboplatin, cisplatin"]
human_relevance_statement: This study in dogs with bone cancer provides a backbone of information that helps test new approaches for this rare human cancer. Researchers can use this study to compare new approaches to see if they provide benefit over a standard of care.

relevant_human_pathways: ["Receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK), TP53 and cell-cycle checkpoint, neural stem cell development."]
human_relevance_statement: Glioma is incurable and affects individuals in the prime of their lives. Gliomas in dogs carry many similarities and offer a unique opportunity to gain new insights. This study is important as it identified new therapy targets and delineated mutational processes, with the potential to result in new treatment modalities for an underserved pediatric patient population.

relevant_human_cancer: ["B Cell Lymhoma, Melanoma, Osteosarcoma, Pulmonary Neoplasms, T Cell Lymphoma"]
human_relevance_statement: Pet dogs can play a key role in advancing human cancer research. By studying five types of cancer in dogs—melanoma, bone cancer, lung cancer, and two types of lymphoma—we uncovered strong genetic similarities between canine and human cancers. Using lab experiments and computer analysis, we identified cancer-specific gene patterns and discovered over 150 potential biomarker targets, not yet well-studied in humans. These findings highlight the value of dogs, who naturally develop cancer like people, as important models for understanding the disease in humans.
relevant_human_genes: ["Pulmonary adenocarcinoma: DPYD, PTGIS, ARG2, PDK2, FADS3, TM9SF3, CYBRD1|Melanoma: SGPL1, DENND5A, CAMSAP2, DOCK7, KIF1B, KIF21A | Osteosarcoma: ROBO1, SPON1, C1QTNF6, BAG2, RCN3, SEC23A | B-Cell Lymphoma: UTP20, RPLP0, RPS7, RPS5, RPL10A, RPS23, RPL35|T-Cell Lymphoma: PIK3CD, EHMT1, SETD3, KMT2B, NFATC2, SLA, KMT2B, BAZ1B"]

relevant_human_pathways: ["epithelial-mesenchymal transition pathways in sarcomatoid subtype bladder cancer"]
human_relevance_statement: Muscle invasive bladder cancer in humans is a heterogeneous disease that is very difficult to treat and is associated with poor survival. Bladder cancer in dogs is a spontaneously occurring cancer that closely resembles human muscle invasive bladder cancer and has been demonstrated to be a great pre-clinical model for the disease. This study is the first that developed 3-dimensional cancer cell lines derived from dogs with bladder cancer which will be useful to screen possible novel drugs before taking them into clinical trials in dogs and subsequently, people with bladder cancer. With this study, we have shown that canine bladder cancer organoids closely represent their parent tumors. Importantly, we also show that the canine bladder cancer organoids match important carcinogenesis pathways of human bladder cancer,  including basal and luminal molecular subtypes, as shown in transcriptomic pathway upregulations (such as epithelial-mesenchymal transition pathways in the most aggressive and invasive tumors), and important somatic mutations such as p53, PIK3CA, KDM6A and KRAS.

This study is important because it helps us know more about bone cancer, specifically osteosarcoma and how it spreads and becomes fatal. The study is focused on osteosarcoma in dogs, a cancer that is very similar to human osteosarcoma. The dog studies are expected to help scientists lean how to improve outcomes for osteosarcoma patients.

relevant_human_pathways: ["Transcriptional regulation by RUNX2, transcriptional regulation by TP53, Transcriptional regulation of pluripotent stem cells"]
human_relevance_statement: This study allowed us to identify how osteosarcomas from dogs and humans use gene methylation, an important epigenetic mechanism, to achieve comparable biological behavior despite their vastly different mutational landscapes. Furthermore, it enabled us to uncover how the tumors in both species can evolve to convergent phenotypes using genetic changes in one species and epigenetic changes in the other.

human_relevance_statement: This study identifies the genetic mutations in a panel of canine cancer cell lines that are similar to those found in human cancers.  By understanding these genetic similarities, researchers can use canine models to test new targeted therapies and drug combinations, improving the success rate of human clinical trials and advancing cancer treatment.

human_relevance_statement: This research is important for human health because it helps scientists understand which genetic changes and immune responses are linked to better survival in bone cancer, using dogs as a model since they get a similar form of the disease. These discoveries could lead to better treatments and new ways to boost the body’s immune system to fight osteosarcoma in people.

relevant_human_pathways: ["p53 signaling, MAP kinase, PI3 Kinase, Receptor tyrosine kinase signaling, fusion gene signaling"]
human_relevance_statement: This study provides a detailed genetic characterization of canine soft tissue sarcomas (STS), revealing mutations and gene fusions that closely parallel those found in human STS. By identifying shared molecular drivers and potential therapeutic targets, such as PDGFB fusions predicting sensitivity to PDGFR inhibitors, the study supports the use of naturally occurring canine tumors as models to develop and test precision-based cancer therapies.
relevant_human_genes: ["P53, MSH3, MSH6, KMT2D, KMT2C, SETD2, PIK3CA, PDGFB, RB1, RAD21, POT1, SPRED1, MAP3K1, EGFR, HRAS"]

human_relevance_statement: About 20% of breast cancers in humans are basal-like, a subtype that is often triple-negative and difficult to treat, and an effective translational model for basal-like breast cancer is currently lacking and urgently needed.  The study shows that about 40% mammary cancers in dogs molecularly represent basal-like breast cancer, and could serve as a valid translational model of this devastating breast cancer subtype.

This study is important because it leverages tumors that naturally occur in dogs to improve the lives of both humans and dogs. Dogs get this bone cancer much more frequently than humans, and it looks indistinguishable from the human disease. By characterizing the mutations in the dog, we will use this information to compare with the human disease and improve the lives of both species.

This study in dogs with bone cancer tested a new inhaled immune therapy that activates natural killer (NK) cells, an important part of the body’s defense against cancer. Because NK cells are difficult to study in humans and mice, this research in dogs helps scientists better understand how to use these cells to treat hard-to-cure cancers in people.

This study is important because it provides extensive new information which is being used to improve the treatment for cancer caused by a certain defect in a gene called BRAF. This defect is a major cause of deadly melanoma and colon cancer in humans.

This study is important because it helps scientists understand one of the important things that makes urinary bladder cancer spread throughout the body and kill humans. With more than half a million people living with bladder cancer in the US, it is critical to figure out what makes the cancer become life-threatening and how to stop this.

This study is important because it shows how much better it is to find and treat bladder cancer as early as possible. In this study in dogs, the treatment worked twice as well as it normally would because the cancer was found when it was just starting to form. Finding bladder cancer early will undoubtedly contribute to healthy aging in people afflicted with this cancer.

This study is important because it reveals important mechanisms at the molecular level that make bladder cancer form and grow. By understanding how these mechanisms work, scientists can develop ways to stop bladder cancer.
